title: International Press Covers Sheinbaum's Barcelona Summit Amid Mexico-Spain Diplomatic Thaw
detail: International and Spanish-language media provided extensive coverage on April 11 of Sheinbaum's announced April 18 trip to Barcelona for the Global Progressive Mobilisation (GPM) summit. La Jornada, El Diario (España), and other outlets highlighted that the visit will mark the first Mexican presidential visit to Spain in eight years — repairing a diplomatic rift that AMLO had maintained over historical grievances related to the Spanish conquest. The summit will bring together Latin American progressive leaders (Lula, Petro, Orsi) alongside European Socialist leaders (Sánchez, Costa) to discuss coordinated responses to rising right-wing governments globally. Catalan public broadcaster RAB confirmed the participation list and described the meeting as the inaugural session of a new permanent international progressive coordination platform. Analysts noted that Sheinbaum's attendance signals both a return to traditional Mexican multilateralism after AMLO's relative isolationism, and a deliberate effort to diversify Mexico's international relationships beyond the fraught relationship with Trump's Washington. The summit platform was originally proposed by former Chilean President Gabriel Boric.

media.caption: Sheinbaum set for Barcelona: first Mexico-Spain presidential visit in 8 years

title: Trump's 100% Section 232 Pharmaceutical Tariffs Raise Mexico Concerns Ahead of July 31 Implementation
detail: Trade analysts and Mexican health sector representatives intensified scrutiny on April 11 of President Trump's April 2 proclamation imposing 100% Section 232 tariffs on patented pharmaceutical imports and active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs), effective July 31, 2026. Mexico, which has a growing pharmaceutical manufacturing sector and is a significant exporter of generic and branded drugs to the U.S., is not exempt from the tariff — unlike the special carve-outs offered to the United Kingdom (10%) and some EU nations (15%). Companies that have approved onshoring plans to move manufacturing to the U.S. will face a reduced 20% rate from September 29, 2026; those entering MFN pharmaceutical pricing agreements receive a 0% rate through January 20, 2029. Mexico's USMCA exemption — which covers the 10% Section 122 general tariffs — does not appear to apply to the new Section 232 pharmaceutical proclamation. The Mexican Secretariat of Economy signaled it would seek clarification on whether USMCA dispute mechanisms could be invoked. The tariffs specifically exclude generic pharmaceuticals, biosimilars, and orphan drugs — which constitute a significant portion of Mexico's pharma export portfolio — somewhat limiting the immediate damage, but the signal remains a concern for the broader nearshoring pharmaceutical investment thesis.

media.caption: Trump prepares pharmaceutical tariffs of up to 100%
